en.extract:
The collared falconet (Microhierax caerulescens) is a species of bird of prey in the family Falconidae.
It is found in the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia, ranging across Bangladesh, Bhutan, Cambodia, India, Laos, Myanmar, Nepal, Thailand, Malaysia, and Vietnam.
Its natural habitat is temperate forest, often on the edges of broadleaf forest.
It is 18 cm (7.1 in) long. Rapid wingbeats are interspersed with long glides. When perched, it is described as being "rather shrikelike".


Taxonomy and systematics
The first description by a European ornithologist was published by George Edwards in 1750, as "the little black and orange colour'd Indian hawk". It was from a specimen that had been collected in Bengal and sent to the king's physician, Richard Mead. In 1758 the Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus used the illustration and description by Edwards to formally describe the species under the binomial name Falco cærulescens. In 1760 the French naturalist Mathurin Jacques Brisson also used Edwards' publication to describe le Faucon de Bengale. Although the white collar was not mentioned, the English ornithologist Richard Bowdler Sharpe believed that this was the collared falconet.  Richard Bowdler Sharpe introduced the genus Microhierax in 1874, from the Greek μικρόσ ἱεραξ meaning "tiny hawk".


Description
The collared falconet is a very small falcon, shrike-like in shape, mainly pied and having bold white supercilia and collar, with relatively heavy double-toothed bill. It has shortish wings, a medium-length tail, strong half-feathered legs, and powerful feet. It perches conspicuously on top or edge of tree or bush, often on dead twig, frequently bobbing head and slowly moving tail up and down; wing-tips less than half down tail. Sexes are similar.


Distribution
Mainland southeast Asia: Himalayan foothills of north India (northern Uttar Pradesh northwest to Kumaun, and Sikkim, Bengal, mainly northern Assam) and of Nepal and Bhutan, and from Burma (central and east, south to Tenasserim), Thailand (northwest and west, but not peninsular, also in strip east of central plains), Laos (central and south), Cambodia (especially north), and Vietnam.


Habitat
Open deciduous forest, clearings and edges in evergreen forest, abandoned hill cultivation with some trees; often near water. Mostly 200–800 m (660–2,620 ft), fairly regularly to 1,700 m (5,600 ft).


References

The '''collared falconet''' ('''''Microhierax caerulescens''''') is a [[species]] of [[bird of prey]] in the family [[Falconidae]].

It is found in the [[Indian subcontinent]] and [[Southeast Asia]], ranging across Bangladesh, Bhutan, Cambodia, India, Laos, Myanmar, Nepal, Thailand, Malaysia, and Vietnam.
Its natural [[habitat]] is [[temperate forest]], often on the edges of broadleaf forest.

It is {{convert|18|cm|abbr=on}} long. Rapid wingbeats are interspersed with long glides. When perched, it is described as being "rather shrikelike".

== Taxonomy and systematics ==
The first description by a European ornithologist was published by [[George Edwards (naturalist)|George Edwards]] in 1750, as "the little black and orange colour'd Indian hawk". It was from a specimen that had been collected in Bengal and sent to the king's physician, [[Richard Mead]].<ref name=edwards>{{cite book|last1=Edwards | author-link=George Edwards (naturalist) |first1=George|title=A Natural History of (Uncommon) Birds | volume=Part 3|year=1750 | location=London|chapter=The little Black and Orange colour'd Indian Hawk | at=Plate 108 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/50196261 }}</ref> In 1758 the Swedish naturalist [[Carl Linnaeus]] used the illustration and description by Edwards to [[Species description|formally describe]] the species under the [[binomial nomenclature|binomial name]] ''Falco cærulescens''.<ref>{{cite book | last=Linnaeus | first=C. | author-link=Carl Linnaeus | year=1758 | title= Systema Naturæ per regna tria naturae, secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis, Volume 1| volume=v.1 | edition=10th | page=88 | publisher=Holmiae:Laurentii Salvii  | language=la | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/726995 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Cottrell | editor2-first=G. William | year=1979 | title=Check-list of Birds of the World. Volume 1 | edition=2nd | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=399 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/16109039 }}</ref> In 1760 the French naturalist [[Mathurin Jacques Brisson]] also used Edwards' publication to describe ''{{lang|fr|le Faucon de Bengale}}''.<ref name="Sharpe1874" /><ref>{{ cite book | last=Brisson | first=Mathurin Jacques | author-link=Mathurin Jacques Brisson | year=1760 | title=Ornithologie, ou, Méthode contenant la division des oiseaux en ordres, sections, genres, especes & leurs variétés: Supplement d'Ornithologie | page=20 | place=Paris | publisher=Jean-Baptiste Bauche | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/36211618 }}</ref> Although the white collar was not mentioned, the English ornithologist [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe]] believed that this was the collared falconet.<ref name="Sharpe1874" />  [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe]] introduced the [[genus]] ''[[Microhierax]]'' in 1874, from the Greek {{lang|el|μικρόσ ἱεραξ}} meaning "tiny hawk".<ref name="Sharpe1874">{{cite book|last1=Sharpe|first1=R. Bowdler|author-link1=Richard Bowdler Sharpe|title=Volume I: Catalogue of the Accipitres or Diurnal Birds of Prey in the Collection of the British Museum|date=1874|publisher=Trustees of the British Museum |location=London|pages=366–369|chapter=Microhierax|series=Catalogue of the Birds in the British Museum | chapter-url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8307902}}</ref>

== Description ==
The collared falconet is a very small falcon, shrike-like in shape, mainly pied and having bold white supercilia and collar, with relatively heavy double-toothed bill. It has shortish wings, a medium-length tail, strong half-feathered legs, and powerful feet. It perches conspicuously on top or edge of tree or bush, often on dead twig, frequently bobbing head and slowly moving tail up and down; wing-tips less than half down tail. Sexes are similar.

== Distribution == 
Mainland southeast Asia: Himalayan foothills of north India (northern [[Uttar Pradesh]] northwest to [[Kumaon division|Kumaun]], and [[Sikkim]], [[Bengal]], mainly northern [[Assam]]) and of Nepal and Bhutan, and from Burma (central and east, south to [[Tenasserim Division|Tenasserim]]), Thailand (northwest and west, but not peninsular, also in strip east of central plains), Laos (central and south), Cambodia (especially north), and Vietnam.

== Habitat == 
Open deciduous forest, clearings and edges in evergreen forest, abandoned hill cultivation with some trees; often near water. Mostly {{convert|200|–|800|m|abbr=on}}, fairly regularly to {{convert|1700|m|abbr=on}}.

zh.extract:
红腿小隼（学名：Microhierax caerulescens）为隼科小隼属的鸟类。多见于森林边缘以及很可能筑巢于树洞。该物种的模式产地在孟加拉国。
分布于印度次大陆和东南亚，于孟加拉、不丹、柬埔寨、印度、老挝、缅甸、尼泊尔、泰国、马来西亚和越南均有分布。栖息在温带森林中，常见于阔叶林的边缘。体长18厘米，飞行速度极快，停在树枝上的姿态酷似伯劳。


保护
中国国家重点保护动物等级：二级


亚种
红腿小隼云南亚种（学名：Microhierax caerulescens burmanicus）。在中国大陆，分布于云南等地。该物种的模式产地在缅甸Thayetmyo。
